name: Attack-Surface Reduction
def: Systematically removing or disabling features, entry points, privileges, and exposed code so there is simply less for an attacker to target.
problem: Every enabled feature, open port, dependency, and granted permission is a potential way in; defaults are usually maximal, not minimal.
mechanism: Disable unused services/ports, strip unneeded packages and dependencies, minimise exposed APIs and parameters, drop privileges, and turn off optional features. Each removal eliminates a class of vulnerabilities outright rather than mitigating it.
benefits: Eliminates whole vulnerability classes by removal
Fewer components to patch and monitor
Cheaper, more durable than per-bug fixes
limits: Can conflict with feature / convenience demands
Requires knowing what is actually used
Ongoing effort as the system grows
failures: Re-enabling debug endpoints / default accounts in production
Dependency creep silently re-expanding the surface
whenNot: Never a bad idea, but balance against required functionality
myths: "More security tools = more security" — often the opposite of reducing what can be attacked
